body.label: thm:disjoint-balls

hypothesis.assumption: (X,d) is a metric space and x, y are two distinct points in X with d(x,y) = \varepsilon > 0.

body.claim: The open balls B(x, \varepsilon/3) and B(y, \varepsilon/3) do not intersect; that is, B(x, \varepsilon/3) \cap B(y, \varepsilon/3) = \varnothing.

proof.assumption: There exists a point z in the intersection B(x, \varepsilon/3) \cap B(y, \varepsilon/3).

proof.statement: Let z \in X be such that z \in B(x,\varepsilon/3) and z \in B(y,\varepsilon/3).

proof.proof_method: By the definition of the open balls B(x,\varepsilon/3) and B(y,\varepsilon/3).
Triangle inequality for the metric d.
From d(x,y) \le d(x,z) + d(z,y) and the bounds d(x,z) < \varepsilon/3, d(z,y) < \varepsilon/3.
Using d(x,y) = \varepsilon and \varepsilon > 0, divide the inequality d(x,y) < 2\varepsilon/3 by \varepsilon.
Basic arithmetic ordering on real numbers.

proof.claim: The intersection B(x, \varepsilon/3) \cap B(y, \varepsilon/3) is empty; hence B(x, \varepsilon/3) and B(y, \varepsilon/3) do not intersect.
